does: This paper identifies governance functions that advanced AI systems require at an international level and proposes four institutional models to address them. The authors analyze how international institutions could support both the benefits and risk management of frontier AI, cataloging functions ranging from facilitating access to AI systems to establishing international safety standards. They ground their proposals in precedents from existing international organizations, developing concrete institutional designs including a Commission on Frontier AI for expert consensus-building and an Advanced AI Governance Organization for setting international standards. The paper situates these mechanisms within the broader context of global coordination challenges posed by AI development, examining how international collaboration can simultaneously unlock sustainable development applications while managing risks from powerful general-purpose systems.
connects: This work extends recent policy discussions around AI governance that emerged following rapid advances in large language models and foundation models. It builds on earlier analyses of AI's dual-use potential and global externalities, connecting to broader literature on international regulatory coordination and precedents in fields like nuclear energy, climate, and biosecurity. The paper engages with ongoing debates about whether AI development should be centralized or distributed, drawing on governance frameworks developed for other transformative technologies. It responds to growing concerns about misalignment between different nations' AI development trajectories and extends foundational work on AI safety standards, institutional design for emerging technologies, and the role of international bodies in managing systemic risks.
enables: This framework enables future work on institutional design for AI governance at unprecedented scales. It points toward the practical development of international safety standards, shared evaluation protocols for advanced AI systems, and coordination mechanisms between nations with different regulatory approaches. The proposed models could facilitate more sophisticated approaches to AI access in developing nations, reducing global inequality in AI benefits. The paper supports subsequent efforts to establish actual institutions modeled on these designs, suggesting pathways for harmonizing national regulations and creating binding international agreements on AI safety practices. It also opens questions about enforcement mechanisms, verification protocols, and how institutions could adapt as AI capabilities evolve, setting up research into adaptive governance and real-time international coordination frameworks for rapidly advancing technologies.
